title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
The code chunk below uses [p_load()](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/pacman/versions/0.5.1/topics/p_load) of pacman package to check if tidyverse packages are installed in this computer. If they are,then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)



exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")



ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = fct_infreq(RACE))) + 
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
The code chunk below uses [p_load()](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/pacman/versions/0.5.1/topics/p_load) of pacman package to check if tidyverse packages are installed in this computer. If they are,then they will be launched into R.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text: exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(x = fct_infreq(RACE))) + 
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: R Graphics
text:
R Graphics

exam_data$MATHS <- as.numeric(exam_data$MATHS)
hist(exam_data$MATHS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: ggplot
text:
ggplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="grey") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Call the ggplot() function
text:
Call the ggplot() function

ggplot(data=exam_data)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Adds the aesthetic element
text:
Adds the aesthetic element

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot a bar chart using geom_bar()
text:
Plot a bar chart using geom_bar()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot a dot plot using geom_dotplot()
text:
Plot a dot plot using geom_dotplot()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Create a simple histogram using geom_histogram()
text:
Create a simple histogram using geom_histogram()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()
text:
Modifying a geometric object by changing geom()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="light blue")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()
text:
Modifying a geometric object by changing aes()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot the distribution of Maths scores using geom-density()
text:
Plot the distribution of Maths scores using geom-density()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_density()  








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot boxplots using geom_boxplot()
text:
Plot boxplots using geom_boxplot()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot() 








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot the distribution of Maths score by gender using geom_violin
text:
Plot the distribution of Maths score by gender using geom_violin


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_violin()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot scatterplot using geom_point()
text:
Plot scatterplot using geom_point()


exam_data$ENGLISH <- as.numeric(exam_data$ENGLISH)

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 20))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point()
text:
Plot the data points on the boxplots by using both geom_boxplot() and geom_point()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Add mean values by using stat_summary()
text:
Add mean values by using stat_summary()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = "point",       
               fun = "mean",         
               colour ="red",        
               size=4)               








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun="mean",           
             colour="red",          
             size=4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Adding a best fit curve on a scatterplot
text:
Adding a best fit curve on a scatterplot

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              linewidth=0.5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Working with facet_wrap()
text:
Working with facet_wrap()

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
    facet_wrap(~ CLASS)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot a trellis plot using facet_grid()
text:
Plot a trellis plot using facet_grid()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_grid(~ CLASS) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 40))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Flip the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip()
text:
Flip the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using coord_flip()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip()

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Fix both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100
text:
Fix both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
section: Plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray()
text:
Plot a horizontal bar chart using theme_gray()


ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_gray()








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()








ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
This exercise focuses on mastering ggplot2 extensions to create professional, publication-quality graphics with precise label placement and styling.
Let’s learn how to use ggrepel, ggthemes, and hrbrthemes for enhanced visuals, and patchwork to combine multiple plots into a single composite figure.

In this part we install the tools and import the data.

This is the code chunk to install the required libraries.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse) 


This part we still use the same exam data as Hands-on Exercise 1.

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")


Adding labels to a graph is hard when there are too many data points.The following is an example of using ggplot to display the relationship between Math score and English score of the exam data set.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = NAME), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")







Apparently too much labels are displayed that we can not see the data at all. So we use ggrepel instead, ggrepel is an extension of ggplot2 package which provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text as in our examples on the right.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = NAME), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")








ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void()
Let’s try theme_gray() first :

exam_data$MATHS <- as.numeric(as.character(exam_data$MATHS))
exam_data$ENGLISH <- as.numeric(as.character(exam_data$ENGLISH))
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 







Next try theme_bw() :

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_bw() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 







Try theme_minimal() :

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_minimal() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 








The above themes look similar,next we try ‘ggplot2’ themes ,which look of plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
The Economist theme:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()







The Fivethirtyeight theme:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()








The hrbrthemes package gives a simple chart style that improves how text looks, including label positions and fonts.

 ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()







The second goal is to improve productivity in a production workflow. This workflow is where hrbrthemes should be used. You can read this vignette to learn more.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")








TipWhat can we learn from the code chunk above?

axis_title_size argument is used to increase the font size of the axis title to 18,
base_size argument is used to increase the default axis label to 15, and
grid argument is used to remove the x-axis grid lines.

Sometimes we need to use multiple graphs to tell a clear and interesting story. Some ggplot2 extensions provide functions to combine several graphs into one figure. We try in the following code chunks.
First, we assign the plots to variables.
This is p1:

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p1







This is p2:

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p2







This is p3:

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

p3








Now we try to combine p1,p2 and p3.
Patchwork package has a very simple syntax where we can create layouts super easily. Here’s the general syntax that combines:

Two-Column Layout using the Plus Sign +.
Parenthesis () to create a subplot group.
Two-Row Layout using the Division Sign /

First,we try to combine two graphs using +, the code is very simple:

p1 + p2








Then we try to combine three graphs, we can use / and | to control the layout:

(p1 / p2) | p3








To help identify subplots in the text, patchwork also provides automatic tagging, as shown in the figure below.

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')








Besides arranging plots side by side, patchwork also provides the inset_element() function. It allows us to place one or more plots or graphic elements on top of or below another plot.

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)








Then we try to combine graphs using different themes,this makes the combined graph more professional.

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()







We can try different themes:

patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_fivethirtyeight()

text:

               tidyverse) 


This part we still use the same exam data as Hands-on Exercise 1.

text:

              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = NAME), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +

                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")







Apparently too much labels are displayed that we can not see the data at all. So we use ggrepel instead, ggrepel is an extension of ggplot2 package which provides geoms for ggplot2 to repel overlapping text as in our examples on the right.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = NAME), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void()
Let’s try theme_gray() first :

exam_data$MATHS <- as.numeric(as.character(exam_data$MATHS))
exam_data$ENGLISH <- as.numeric(as.character(exam_data$ENGLISH))
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 







Next try theme_bw() :

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +

  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 







Try theme_minimal() :

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +

  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") 








The above themes look similar,next we try ‘ggplot2’ themes ,which look of plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
The Economist theme:

             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,

                 fill="grey90") +

  theme_economist()







The Fivethirtyeight theme:

             aes(x = MATHS)) +

                 boundary = 100,

                 fill="grey90") +

  theme_fivethirtyeight()








The hrbrthemes package gives a simple chart style that improves how text looks, including label positions and fonts.

 ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()







The second goal is to improve productivity in a production workflow. This workflow is where hrbrthemes should be used. You can read this vignette to learn more.

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")








TipWhat can we learn from the code chunk above?

axis_title_size argument is used to increase the font size of the axis title to 18,
base_size argument is used to increase the default axis label to 15, and
grid argument is used to remove the x-axis grid lines.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Sometimes we need to use multiple graphs to tell a clear and interesting story. Some ggplot2 extensions provide functions to combine several graphs into one figure. We try in the following code chunks.
First, we assign the plots to variables.
This is p1:

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p1







This is p2:

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p2







This is p3:

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +

p3








Now we try to combine p1,p2 and p3.
Patchwork package has a very simple syntax where we can create layouts super easily. Here’s the general syntax that combines:

Two-Column Layout using the Plus Sign +.
Parenthesis () to create a subplot group.
Two-Row Layout using the Division Sign /

First,we try to combine two graphs using +, the code is very simple:

p1 + p2








Then we try to combine three graphs, we can use / and | to control the layout:

(p1 / p2) | p3

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02,

title: ISSS608-VAA
text:
Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Application homepage. In this website, you will find my coursework prepared for this course. All materials and assignments are based on the course taught by Professor Tin Seong Kam.





Data visualization is an essential skill for professional data analysts. It enables analytical results to be presented in a clear and intuitive way, allowing even those without a strong background in data analysis to quickly grasp key insights.
Let’s start this exciting journey together. I hope everyone who visits this website can gain something valuable and meaningful from it.
